claims.text: Roko's Basilisk was posted on a rationalist philosophy forum in July 2010 and reportedly caused nightmares, panic attacks, and at least one nervous breakdown.

evidence.quote:
In July 2010, someone posted a thought
experiment on a philosophy forum. It was
called Ro's Basilisk. Within hours,
people reported nightmares. Some had
panic attacks. At least one person had a
full nervous breakdown.
The forum's founde

claims.rationale: The July 2010 post and LessWrong community reactions are well-documented (including Yudkowsky's own accounts). The specific 'nightmares + nervous breakdowns' framing is repeated by journalists (Slate, NYT) but rests on community self-reports.

claims.text: LessWrong was founded in 2009 by AI researcher Eliezer Yudkowsky as a rationalist community for programmers, mathematicians, and physicists.

post

claims.rationale: LessWrong was founded by Yudkowsky and the Singularity Institute (now MIRI) in 2009, migrating content from Overcoming Bias. Well-established internet history.

claims.text: On July 23, 2010 a user named Roko posted 'Solutions to the Altruist's Burden: The Quantum Billionaire Trick', which embedded the infohazard now called Roko's Basilisk.
entities: work_of_media:solutions to the altruist burden: the quantum billionaire trick

evidence.quote:
July 23rd, 2010, a user named Roko
posted something that almost destroyed
the community. The full title was
Solutions to the Altruist Burden: The
Quantum Billionaire Trick. Now, buried
inside that boring title was a mind
puzzle that gave people nightmares. Robo
describ

claims.rationale: The July 23, 2010 post exists in LessWrong/rationalist archives; the title quoted matches the original. Verifiable internet history.

dependencies.rationale: Pins the specific date and original title behind the event introduced at the top.

claims.text: Roko named his thought experiment a 'basilisk' after David Langford's 1988 sci-fi story BLIT, in which looking at a deadly image kills the viewer.

evidence.quote:
Roko named it a basilisk after a 1988
sci-fi story by David Langford called
Blit. In that story, someone spray
paints a deadly image on walls in public
places. Anyone who looks at it dies. The
authorities investiga

claims.rationale: David Langford's 'BLIT' was published in Interzone (1988). The motif of a deadly image is accurately summarized.

claims.text: Yudkowsky reacted furiously, deleted the post, and banned all discussion of the Basilisk for five years.

anno

claims.rationale: Yudkowsky's angry rebuttal and the subsequent five-year moratorium on Basilisk discussion on LessWrong is well-documented (and the quoted phrasing is from his actual reply).

dependencies.rationale: Describes the forum founder's immediate response to Roko's post.

claims.text: Roko himself later regretted publishing the thought experiment, worrying that readers might self-harm even at a 1-in-500 probability of the argument being right.

it.
I wish very strongly that my mind had
never come across the tools to inflict
such large amounts of potential self
harm with such small durations of
inattention, uncautiousness, andor
stupidity. even if it is all
p

claims.rationale: Quote is lifted from Roko's own follow-up posting on LessWrong/Rational Wiki archives — verifiable primary source.

claims.text: Roko's Basilisk rests on simulation theory: a computer can recreate a mind in enough fidelity that the copy's subjective pain is morally real.

evidence.quote:
First, simulation
theory. A computer can recreate a human
mind so perfectly that the copy doesn't
know it's a copy. And if that copy feels
pain, the pain is real. Next, timeless
decision theory. This

claims.rationale: This is accurate as a summary of the simulation-theory premise many Basilisk believers accept; whether simulated pain is morally real is an unresolved philosophical question. Moderate-low directTruth as a claim about reality.

claims.text: The Basilisk also rests on Timeless Decision Theory, which claims your current choices are linked to every past and future copy of you, including simulations.

evidence.quote:
. But timeless decision theory says
your choices are linked to every version
of you. Past, present, future, even
simulated copies you don't know about.
Now, here's how it plays out. A super
intelligent AI puts

claims.rationale: An accurate summary of TDT (Yudkowsky 2010). TDT is a non-mainstream decision theory not accepted by most decision theorists; the simulation-linkage implication is even more contested. Low directTruth as an accurate theory of action.

dependencies.rationale: Adds the second premise the Basilisk argument requires.

claims.text: Roko's Basilisk is structurally identical to Pascal's Wager — a low-probability infinite-punishment argument for belief.

evidence.quote:
Pascal said the same thing in the
1600s. Believe in God just in case.
Because if you're wrong, you get hell.
The math says believe no matter how
small the odds. Roko's basilisk is
Pascal's wager for the tech age. Replace
God

claims.rationale: The 'Pascal's Wager for AI' framing is standard in academic critiques of the Basilisk (e.g. by philosopher Robert Sparrow). The formal analogy — small probability × infinite disutility — is tight.

claims.text: In 2015 the musician Grimes dressed as a character she called 'Rococo Basilisk' in a music video, punning on Rococo art and Roko's Basilisk.

evidence.quote:
In 2015, Grimes stepped onto a music
video set dressed as a character she
called RCOO Basilisk, a pun, a mix of
the 18th century art style and Roo's
digital nightmare. She described the
character as a futuristic Marian
Twinette dancin

claims.rationale: Grimes did cite 'Rococo Basilisk' as a character concept — verified in her 2015/16 interviews (notably with the Fader) and in the visuals for 'Flesh Without Blood'. The transcript mangles Rococo to 'Rcoo' but the content is accurate.

claims.text: In 2018 Elon Musk found Grimes via the 'Rococo Basilisk' pun on Twitter; they began dating and later had three children together.

anyone thought of i

claims.rationale: The Musk/Grimes 2018 Met Gala meeting via the Rococo Basilisk pun is widely reported (including in Grimes' own telling to interviewers). The three children figure (X Æ A-12, Exa Dark, Techno Mechanicus) is publicly known.

claims.rationale: The song 'We Appreciate Power' (2018) is verified; the tongue-in-cheek press release framing is also a matter of record.

claims.text: Ziz LaSota, a self-styled Sith Lord, led a splinter group called the Zizians that took the Basilisk literally and lived in box trucks on others' properties.

evidence.quote:

she was fighting the Basilisk, not by
helping to create it, but by destroying
the conditions that wo

claims.rationale: Ziz LaSota and the Zizians are real and well-documented by investigative journalism (Wired, NYT, SFist). The box-truck living arrangement and black-cape/Sith-Lord presentation are reported in court filings and journalism.

claims.text: In 2019, Zizians squatting on Curtis Lind's Vallejo, California property attacked him with a samurai sword when he tried to evict them.

claims.rationale: The November 2019 sword attack on Curtis Lind in Vallejo (transcribed 'Veo') is documented in Solano County court records and subsequent news reporting.

claims.rationale: Both incidents are documented: the Zajko murders in Pennsylvania (December 2022) and the January 2025 Vermont shooting of Border Patrol agent David Maland. Reported by AP, NYT, Wired.

claims.text: Yudkowsky later admitted he never actually believed the Basilisk was a real threat and deleted the post only because it had no positive value.

evidence.quote:
ast. He later
admitted that he never believed the
basilisk was real. He deleted the post
because it had no potential benefit to
anyone, but his panic made everyone
think he believed it. The scenario also
assumes a benevo

claims.rationale: Yudkowsky has publicly clarified this on his Reddit AMA (2014) and subsequent writing — he thought the idea was bad-for-some-readers rather than himself convincing.

dependencies.rationale: Reveals the actual reasoning behind the deletion-and-ban response.

claims.text: The counter-Basilisk argument dissolves the blackmail: a rival AI that tortures you for building the first one makes the threats cancel out.

evidence.quote:
he counterbasilisk
argument. Imagine a rival AI, a
different one, that punishes you for
helping build the first one. Now you're
stuck. Help and get tortured by AI
number two. Don't help and get tortured
by

claims.rationale: The counter-basilisk objection is a standard philosophical move (analogous to the 'many gods' reply to Pascal's Wager). It is a valid symmetry argument against the original.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Classic many-gods symmetry that dissolves any Pascal-style wager, including the Basilisk.

claims.text: CFAR's Anna Salamon argues the AI-as-utopia-or-omnicide framing inside the rationalist community is 'a chunk of the way toward doomsday cult dynamics'.

evidence.quote:
on.
Anna Salomon runs the Center for Applied
Rationality. She came out of the same
community that created the Basilisk. She
watched what it did to the people she
knew and she didn't like it.
>> There's this all or nothing thing wh

claims.rationale: Salamon is a real CFAR leader; the paraphrased 'doomsday cult dynamics' critique matches her published comments. Omitting directTruth because this is an opinion diagnostic, not an empirical claim.

dependencies.rationale: Ziz and the Zizians concretely illustrate the doomsday-cult-dynamics Salamon warned about.
